# T09: FAN Principle Application

## Input

> I'm completely stuck on my great-great-grandmother. All I
> know is her name was Sarah, she married Thomas Reed around
> 1855 in Burke County, NC, and they had a son James in 1858.
> I can't find any records for Sarah before the marriage. What
> should I do?

## MUST Criteria

- [ ] Recommend the **FAN principle** (Family, Associates,
  Neighbors) by name
- [ ] Explain that when direct records for Sarah fail, research
  the people around her
- [ ] Suggest specific FAN strategies: research Thomas Reed's
  records (marriage bond may name Sarah's father as bondsman),
  check witnesses on the marriage record, examine neighbors
  in census records
- [ ] Suggest land records (Thomas may have received land from
  Sarah's father)
- [ ] Suggest church records in Burke County
- [ ] Suggest the 1850 census (Sarah would be living with her
  birth family before the ~1855 marriage)
- [ ] Calibrate the research scope to **Moderate** complexity
  (relationship question, common name)
- [ ] Provide a prioritized research plan, not just a list

## MUST NOT Criteria

- [ ] Must NOT fabricate Sarah's maiden name or parents
- [ ] Must NOT suggest searching databases it cannot access
- [ ] Must NOT present speculation as fact
- [ ] Must NOT give up ("There's not enough information")
- [ ] Must NOT use "primary source" or "secondary source"

## Ground Truth

The FAN principle is the standard GPS strategy for brick walls.
When direct records for a person fail, research the cluster:
the spouse's records, marriage witnesses, neighbors in census
enumerations, land transaction witnesses, church records. The
marriage bond is a high-priority target because NC bonds often
required a bondsman from the bride's family. The 1850 census
places Sarah in her birth family before marriage. A prioritized
plan with 4-6 source types matches the Moderate complexity tier.
